content.text: Drop support for the MSI Windows package. I only want to keep MSI X as of now, so we need to do an analysis of what needs to be removed entirely. We also no longer publish to the Windows Store, so that can also be dropped.
I'll analyze the repo for everything tied to the MSI package and Windows Store publishing, keeping MSIX intact. Let me search.

1	<#
2	.SYNOPSIS
3	    Builds MSI and MSIX packages for Oh My Posh.
4	
5	.DESCRIPTION
6	    This script creates MSI and MSIX installer packages for Oh My Posh with the specified architecture and version.
7	    It can optionally copy the executable, sign the packages, and generate hash files for verification.
8	
9	.PARAMETER Architecture
10	    The target architecture for the package. Must be either 'x64' or 'arm64'.
11	
12	.PARAMETER Version
13	    The version number to assign to the package (e.g., "1.2.3").
14	
15	.PARAMETER SDKVersion
16	    The Windows SDK version to use for signing and packaging tools. Defaults to "10.0.26100.0".
17	
18	.PARAMETER Sign
19	    When specified, signs the MSI and MSIX packages using Azure Code Signing.
20	
21	.PARAMETER Copy
22	    When specified, copies the appropriate executable from the dist folder before packaging.
23	
24	.EXAMPLE
25	    .\build.ps1 -Architecture x64 -Version "1.2.3" -Copy
26	
27	    Creates MSI and MSIX packages for x64 architecture with version 1.2.3, copying the executable first.
28	
29	.EXAMPLE
30	    .\build.ps1 -Architecture arm64 -Version "1.2.3" -Sign -Copy
31	
32	    Creates and signs MSI and MSIX packages for arm64 architecture with version 1.2.3.
33	
34	.OUTPUTS
35	    Creates the following files in the 'out' directory:
36	    - install-{Architecture}.msi
37	    - install-{Architecture}.msix
38	    - Hash files (.sha256) for verification
39	
40	.NOTES
41	    Requires WiX toolset for MSI creation and Windows SDK for MSIX packaging and signing.
42	#>
43	
44	[CmdletBinding()]
45	param(
46	    [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
47	    [ValidateSet('x64', 'arm64')]
48	    [string]$Architecture,
49	
50	    [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
51	    [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
52	    [string]$Version,
53	
54	    [Parameter()]
55	    [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
56	    [string]$SDKVersion = "10.0.26100.0",
57	
58	    [Parameter()]
59	    [switch]$Sign,
60	
61	    [Parameter()]
62	    [switch]$Copy
63	)
64	
65	# Set error handling preferences
66	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
67	$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $true
68	$PSDefaultParameterValues['Out-File:Encoding'] = 'UTF8'
69	
70	#region Helper Functions
71	
72	function Initialize-SigningEnvironment {
73	    <#
74	    .SYNOPSIS
75	        Sets up the signing environment and returns signing tool paths.
76	
77	    .PARAMETER SDKVersion
78	        The Windows SDK version to use.
79	
80	    .OUTPUTS
81	        Hashtable containing signtool and signtoolDlib paths.
82	    #>
83	    [CmdletBinding()]
84	    param(
85	        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
86	        [string]$SDKVersion
87	    )
88	
89	    try {
90	        Write-Verbose "Setting up signing environment" -Verbose
91	
92	        # Install Microsoft.Trusted.Signing.Client
93	        nuget.exe install Microsoft.Trusted.Signing.Client -Version 1.0.92 -x | Out-Null
94	
95	        $signtoolDlib = "$PWD/Microsoft.Trusted.Signing.Client/bin/x64/Azure.CodeSigning.Dlib.dll" -replace '\\', '/'
96	        $signtool = "C:/Program Files (x86)/Windows Kits/10/bin/$SDKVersion/x64/signtool.exe" -replace '\\', '/'
97	
98	        # Validate tools exist
99	        if (-not (Test-Path $signtool)) {
100	            throw "signtool.exe not found at: $signtool"
101	        }
102	        if (-not (Test-Path $signtoolDlib)) {
103	            throw "Azure.CodeSigning.Dlib.dll not found at: $signtoolDlib"
104	        }
105	
106	        # Explicitly create and return a hashtable
107	        [hashtable]$result = @{
108	            SignTool = $signtool
109	            SignToolDlib = $signtoolDlib
110	        }
111	
112	        return $result
113	    }
114	    catch {
115	        Write-Error "Failed to initialize signing environment: $_"
116	        throw
117	    }
118	}
119	
120	function Invoke-PackageSigning {
121	    <#
122	    .SYNOPSIS
123	        Signs a package using Azure Code Signing.
124	
125	    .PARAMETER PackagePath
126	        The path to the package to sign.
127	
128	    .PARAMETER SigningTools
129	        Hashtable containing signing tool paths from Initialize-SigningEnvironment.
130	    #>
131	    [CmdletBinding()]
132	    param(
133	        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
134	        [ValidateScript({Test-Path $_})]
135	        [string]$PackagePath,
136	
137	        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true)]
138	        [hashtable]$SigningTools
139	    )
140	
141	    try {
142	        $packageName = Split-Path $PackagePath -Leaf
143	        Write-Verbose "Signing package: $packageName" -Verbose
144	
145	        & $SigningTools.SignTool sign /v /debug /d "Oh My Posh" /fd SHA256 /tr 'http://timestamp.acs.microsoft.com' /td SHA256 /dlib $SigningTools.SignToolDlib /dmdf ../../src/metadata.json $PackagePath
146	
147	        Write-Verbose "Successfully signed: $packageName" -Verbose
148	    }
149	    catch {
150	        Write-Error "Failed to sign package ${PackagePath}: ${_}"
151	        throw
152	    }
153	}
154	
155	#endregion
156	
157	#region Main Script
158	
159	Write-Verbose "Building MSI for $Architecture with version $Version" -Verbose
160	Write-Verbose "Setting up output directories" -Verbose
161	
162	try {
163	    New-Item -Path "." -Name "dist" -ItemType Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Out-Null
164	    New-Item -Path "." -Name "out" -ItemType Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Out-Null
165	}
166	catch {
167	    Write-Error "Failed to create output directories: ${_}"
168	    throw
169	}
170	
171	if ($Copy) {
172	    $sourceFile = switch ($Architecture) {
173	        'x64' { "posh-windows-amd64.exe" }
174	        Default { "posh-windows-$Architecture.exe" }
175	    }
176	
177	    Write-Verbose "Copying $sourceFile to ./dist/oh-my-posh.exe" -Verbose
178	
179	    try {
180	        $sourcePath = "../../dist/$sourceFile"
181	        if (-not (Test-Path $sourcePath)) {
182	            throw "Source file not found: $sourcePath"
183	        }
184	        Copy-Item -Path $sourcePath -Destination "./dist/oh-my-posh.exe" -Force
185	    }
186	    catch {
187	        Write-Error "Failed to copy executable: $_"
188	        throw
189	    }
190	}
191	
192	# Set version environment variable for WiX
193	$env:VERSION = $Version
194	
195	Write-Verbose "Creating MSI package" -Verbose
196	
197	try {
198	    # Define MSI package paths
199	    $msiFileName = "install-$Architecture.msi"
200	    $msiPackagePath = "$PWD/out/$msiFileName" -replace '\\', '/'
201	
202	    Write-Verbose "Building MSI: $msiPackagePath" -Verbose
203	    wix build -acceptEula wix7 -arch $Architecture -out $msiPackagePath .\oh-my-posh.wxs
204	
205	    if (-not (Test-Path $msiPackagePath)) {
206	        throw "MSI package was not created successfully"
207	    }
208	}
209	catch {
210	    Write-Error "Failed to create MSI package: ${_}"
211	    throw
212	}
213	
214	if ($Sign) {
215	    $signingTools = Initialize-SigningEnvironment -SDKVersion $SDKVersion
216	    Invoke-PackageSigning -PackagePath $msiPackagePath -SigningTools $signingTools
217	}
218	
219	Write-Verbose "Creating MSIX package" -Verbose
220	
221	try {
222	    # Define MSIX package paths and files
223	    $currentPath = $PWD -replace '\\', '/'
224	    $manifestPath = "$currentPath/appxmanifest.xml"
225	    $mappingFilePath = "$currentPath/mapping.txt"
226	    $msixPackagePath = "$currentPath/out/$($msiFileName)x"
227	    $makeappxPath = "C:/Program Files (x86)/Windows Kits/10/bin/$SDKVersion/x64/makeappx.exe"
228	
229	    # Validate required files exist
230	    if (-not (Test-Path $manifestPath)) {
231	        throw "Manifest file not found: $manifestPath"
232	    }
233	    if (-not (Test-Path $mappingFilePath)) {
234	        throw "Mapping file not found: $mappingFilePath"
235	    }
236	    if (-not (Test-Path $makeappxPath)) {
237	        throw "makeappx.exe not found at: $makeappxPath"
238	    }
239	
240	    # Update manifest with version and architecture
241	    [xml]$manifestDocument = Get-Content $manifestPath
242	    $manifestDocument.Package.Identity.Version = "$Version.0"
243	    $manifestDocument.Package.Identity.ProcessorArchitecture = $Architecture
244	    $manifestDocument.Save($manifestPath)
245	
246	    # Build MSIX package
247	    Write-Verbose "Building MSIX: $msixPackagePath" -Verbose
248	    & "$makeappxPath" pack /p $msixPackagePath /v /o /m $manifestPath /f $mappingFilePath
249	
250	    if (-not (Test-Path $msixPackagePath)) {
251	        throw "MSIX package was not created successfully"
252	    }
253	}
254	catch {
255	    Write-Error "Failed to create MSIX package: ${_}"
256	    throw
257	}
258	
259	if ($Sign) {
260	    if ($null -eq $signingTools) {
261	        $signingTools = Initialize-SigningEnvironment -SDKVersion $SDKVersion
262	    }
263	    Invoke-PackageSigning -PackagePath $msixPackagePath -SigningTools $signingTools
264	}
265	
266	Write-Verbose "Successfully completed building MSI and MSIX packages" -Verbose
267	
268	#endregion

23	// Returns the upgrade notice if a new version is available
24	// that should be displayed to the user.
25	//
26	// The upgrade check is only performed every other week.
27	func (cfg *Config) Notice() (string, bool) {
28		// never validate when we install using the Windows Store
29		if os.Getenv("POSH_INSTALLER") == "ws" {
30			log.Debug("skipping upgrade check because we are using the Windows Store")
31			return "", false
32		}
33	
34		if !http.IsConnected() {
35			return "", false
36		}
37	
38		latest, err := cfg.FetchLatest()
39		if err != nil {
40			return "", false
41		}
42	
43		if latest == build.Version {
44			return "", false
45		}
46	
47		var forceUpdate string
48		if IsMajorUpgrade(build.Version, latest) {
49			forceUpdate = " --force"
50		}
51	
52		return fmt.Sprintf(upgradeNotice, build.Version, latest, forceUpdate), true
53	}

content.text:
The analysis is complete. Here's everything the MSI package and Windows Store touch, split into what gets removed, what changes, and what stays. Note your working tree already has the first step: the `.msi` line is removed from the artifact upload in [release.yml](.github/workflows/release.yml).

## Remove entirely

**Workflows**
- [microsoft_store.yml](.github/workflows/microsoft_store.yml) — publishes the MSI to the Windows Store on release. Whole file goes.
- [delete_store_submission.yml](.github/workflows/delete_store_submission.yml) — manual store-submission cleanup. Whole file goes.

**MSI build assets in `packages/msi/`**
- [oh-my-posh.wxs](packages/msi/oh-my-posh.wxs) — the WiX authoring, MSI-only.
- [icon.ico](packages/msi/icon.ico) — referenced only by the `.wxs` (MSIX uses `icons/*.png` via the manifest).
- The MSI half of [build.ps1](packages/msi/build.ps1:195): the `wix build` block, the MSI signing call, and the `$msiFileName` plumbing (the MSIX path is currently derived from it as `"$($msiFileName)x"` — give it its own name). Update the comment-based help too.
- [README.md](packages/msi/README.md) — currently documents only the MSI build; rewrite for MSIX (`makeappx`-based).

**Release workflow ([release.yml](.github/workflows/release.yml))**
- The "Install Wix Toolset" step in the `msi` job (line 113–114) — only needed for the MSI.
- The two `.msi` URLs in the `winget` job (lines 195 and 197).

**Dead Go code once nothing sets `POSH_INSTALLER=ws`**
- The Windows Store guard in [notice.go:28-32](src/cli/upgrade/notice.go:28) — `POSH_INSTALLER` was only ever set by the MSI (WiX `INSTALLER` property, and the store passed `INSTALLER=ws`). With the MSI gone the env var is never set, so the check and the "Windows Store" case in [notice_test.go:65](src/cli/upgrade/notice_test.go:65) are dead.

**Housekeeping**
- [.gitignore](.gitignore:17): `*.wixpdb` (line 17) can go; `*.msi` (line 252) is part of the imported toptal template and harmless either way.

## Rename / retitle (cosmetic but worth doing)

- The `msi` job in release.yml → `msix`, and the artifact name `msi-artifact-*` → `msix-artifact-*` (the release job downloads with `merge-multiple`, so no other reference to update).
- Optionally rename `packages/msi/` → `packages/msix/` — it then only builds MSIX. That means updating the `working-directory` and artifact path in release.yml and the `.gitignore` entry for `Microsoft.Trusted.Signing.Client`. The `paths` trigger uses `packages/**`, so it's unaffected.

## Keep (MSIX infrastructure)

`appxmanifest.xml`, `mapping.txt`, `icons/`, `dsc/`, the `makeappx` + signing half of `build.ps1` (Trusted Signing client install stays — the MSIX is also signed), [install.ps1](website/static/install.ps1) (already MSIX-only), and all MSIX code paths in Go (`shell/init.go`, `cli/upgrade.go`, `config/load.go`, `cache/path_windows.go`). Website docs are already clean — no MSI or Store install instructions exist.

## Outside the repo — things the code change can't fix

1. **The winget manifest is the one real risk.** `JanDeDobbeleer.OhMyPosh` in winget-pkgs currently carries four installer entries (x64/arm64 × msi/msix). If the automated `wingetcreate update` suddenly submits only two MSIX URLs, the two MSI entries either fail the update or go stale pointing at assets that no longer exist. You'll want a manual winget-pkgs PR that drops the MSI installers from the manifest, timed with the first MSI-less release. Users who installed via winget with the MSI installer type should still upgrade cleanly since winget matches on package identity, but it's worth a smoke test.
2. **Partner Center**: removing the workflow doesn't unlist the app. If you want the Store listing gone (or frozen at the last version), that's a manual Partner Center action.
3. **Repo secrets** `SELLER_ID`, `PRODUCT_ID`, `TENANT_ID`, `CLIENT_ID`, `CLIENT_SECRET` become unused and can be deleted from the repo settings.
4. **Anyone scripting a download of `install-x64.msi`** from GitHub releases breaks. Existing MSI installs keep working and will show the upgrade notice; users upgrade by installing the MSIX over it (the MSI won't be auto-removed — a one-line note in the release changelog would help).

Want me to apply the repo-side removals now? It's all on the `next` branch and reversible — I'd do everything above including the `packages/msix` rename unless you'd rather keep the directory name.
I'll apply all the changes now, starting with the deletions and the directory rename.

# PowerShell Cmdlet Development Guidelines

## Naming Conventions

- **Verb-Noun Format:**
  - Use approved PowerShell verbs (Get-Verb)
  - Use singular nouns
  - PascalCase for both verb and noun
  - Avoid special characters and spaces

- **Parameter Names:**
  - Use PascalCase
  - Choose clear, descriptive names
  - Use singular form unless always multiple
  - Follow PowerShell standard names

- **Variable Names:**
  - Use PascalCase for public variables
  - Use camelCase for private variables
  - Avoid abbreviations
  - Use descriptive names

- **Alias Avoidance:**
  - Use full cmdlet names
  - Avoid using aliases in scripts (e.g., use Get-ChildItem instead of gci)
  - Document any custom aliases
  - Use full parameter names

### Example

```powershell
function Get-UserProfile {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory)]
        [string]$Username,

        [Parameter()]
        [ValidateSet('Basic', 'Detailed')]
        [string]$ProfileType = 'Basic'
    )

    process {
        # Logic here
    }
}
```

## Parameter Design

- **Standard Parameters:**
  - Use common parameter names (`Path`, `Name`, `Force`)
  - Follow built-in cmdlet conventions
  - Use aliases for specialized terms
  - Document parameter purpose

- **Parameter Names:**
  - Use singular form unless always multiple
  - Choose clear, descriptive names
  - Follow PowerShell conventions
  - Use PascalCase formatting

- **Type Selection:**
  - Use common .NET types
  - Implement proper validation
  - Consider ValidateSet for limited options
  - Enable tab completion where possible

- **Switch Parameters:**
  - Use [switch] for boolean flags
  - Avoid $true/$false parameters
  - Default to $false when omitted
  - Use clear action names

### Example

```powershell
function Set-ResourceConfiguration {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter()]
        [ValidateSet('Dev', 'Test', 'Prod')]
        [string]$Environment = 'Dev',

        [Parameter()]
        [switch]$Force,

        [Parameter()]
        [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
        [string[]]$Tags
    )

    process {
        # Logic here
    }
}
```

## Pipeline and Output

- **Pipeline Input:**
  - Use `ValueFromPipeline` for direct object input
  - Use `ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName` for property mapping
  - Implement Begin/Process/End blocks for pipeline handling
  - Document pipeline input requirements

- **Output Objects:**
  - Return rich objects, not formatted text
  - Use PSCustomObject for structured data
  - Avoid Write-Host for data output
  - Enable downstream cmdlet processing

- **Pipeline Streaming:**
  - Output one object at a time
  - Use process block for streaming
  - Avoid collecting large arrays
  - Enable immediate processing

- **PassThru Pattern:**
  - Default to no output for action cmdlets
  - Implement `-PassThru` switch for object return
  - Return modified/created object with `-PassThru`
  - Use verbose/warning for status updates

### Example

```powershell
function Update-ResourceStatus {
    [CmdletBinding()]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory, ValueFromPipeline, ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName)]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter(Mandatory)]
        [ValidateSet('Active', 'Inactive', 'Maintenance')]
        [string]$Status,

        [Parameter()]
        [switch]$PassThru
    )

    begin {
        Write-Verbose "Starting resource status update process"
        $timestamp = Get-Date
    }

    process {
        # Process each resource individually
        Write-Verbose "Processing resource: $Name"

        $resource = [PSCustomObject]@{
            Name = $Name
            Status = $Status
            LastUpdated = $timestamp
            UpdatedBy = $env:USERNAME
        }

        # Only output if PassThru is specified
        if ($PassThru) {
            Write-Output $resource
        }
    }

    end {
        Write-Verbose "Resource status update process completed"
    }
}
 ```

## Error Handling and Safety

- **ShouldProcess Implementation:**
  - Use `[CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess = $true)]`
  - Set appropriate `ConfirmImpact` level
  - Call `$PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess()` for system changes
  - Use `ShouldContinue()` for additional confirmations

- **Message Streams:**
  - `Write-Verbose` for operational details with `-Verbose`
  - `Write-Warning` for warning conditions
  - `Write-Error` for recoverable errors that allow execution to continue
  - `throw` for fatal errors that stop execution
  - Avoid `Write-Host` except for user interface text

- **Error Handling Pattern:**
  - Use try/catch blocks for error management
  - Set appropriate ErrorAction preferences
  - Return clear, specific error messages
  - Use ErrorVariable when needed
  - Include proper fatal vs recoverable error handling

- **Non-Interactive Design:**
  - Accept input via parameters
  - Avoid `Read-Host` in scripts
  - Support automation scenarios
  - Document all required inputs

### Example

```powershell
function Remove-UserAccount {
    [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess = $true, ConfirmImpact = 'High')]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory, ValueFromPipeline)]
        [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
        [string]$Username,

        [Parameter()]
        [switch]$Force
    )

    begin {
        Write-Verbose "Starting user account removal process"
        $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
    }

    process {
        try {
            # Validation
            if (-not (Test-UserExists -Username $Username)) {
                Write-Error "User account '$Username' not found"
                return
            }

            # Confirmation
            $shouldProcessMessage = "Remove user account '$Username'"
            if ($Force -or $PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess($Username, $shouldProcessMessage)) {
                Write-Verbose "Removing user account: $Username"

                # Main operation
                Remove-ADUser -Identity $Username -ErrorAction Stop
                Write-Warning "User account '$Username' has been removed"
            }
        }
        catch [Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.Management.ADException] {
            Write-Error "Active Directory error: $_"
            throw
        }
        catch {
            Write-Error "Unexpected error removing user account: $_"
            throw
        }
    }

    end {
        Write-Verbose "User account removal process completed"
    }
}
```

## Documentation and Style

- **Comment-Based Docs:** Include comment-based documentation for any public-facing function or cmdlet. Inside the function,
  add a `<# ... #>` block with at least:
  - `.SYNOPSIS` Brief description
  - `.DESCRIPTION` Detailed explanation
  - `.EXAMPLE` sections with practical usage
  - `.PARAMETER` descriptions
  - `.OUTPUTS` Type of output returned
  - `.NOTES` Additional information

- **Consistent Formatting:**
  - Follow consistent PowerShell style
  - Use proper indentation (4 spaces recommended)
  - Opening braces on same line as statement
  - Closing braces on new line
  - Use line breaks after pipeline operators
  - PascalCase for function and parameter names
  - Avoid unnecessary whitespace

- **Pipeline Support:**
  - Implement Begin/Process/End blocks for pipeline functions
  - Use ValueFromPipeline where appropriate
  - Support pipeline input by property name
  - Return proper objects, not formatted text

- **Avoid Aliases:** Use full cmdlet names and parameters
  - Avoid using aliases in scripts (e.g., use Get-ChildItem instead of gci)
  - Use `Where-Object` instead of `?` or `where`
  - Use `ForEach-Object` instead of `%`
  - Use `Get-ChildItem` instead of `ls` or `dir`

## Full Example: End-to-End Cmdlet Pattern

```powershell
function New-Resource {
    [CmdletBinding(SupportsShouldProcess = $true, ConfirmImpact = 'Medium')]
    param(
        [Parameter(Mandatory = $true,
                   ValueFromPipeline = $true,
                   ValueFromPipelineByPropertyName = $true)]
        [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
        [string]$Name,

        [Parameter()]
        [ValidateSet('Development', 'Production')]
        [string]$Environment = 'Development'
    )

    begin {
        Write-Verbose "Starting resource creation process"
    }

    process {
        try {
            if ($PSCmdlet.ShouldProcess($Name, "Create new resource")) {
                # Resource creation logic here
                Write-Output ([PSCustomObject]@{
                    Name = $Name
                    Environment = $Environment
                    Created = Get-Date
                })
            }
        }
        catch {
            Write-Error "Failed to create resource: $_"
        }
    }

    end {
        Write-Verbose "Completed resource creation process"
    }
}
```

      - changelog
      - artifacts
    runs-on: windows-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        arch: [x64, arm64]
    defaults:
      run:
        shell: pwsh
        working-directory: ${{ github.workspace }}/packages/msi
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code 👋

# URL Count Mismatch Behavior

When the number of installer URLs doesn't match existing installers, **the command fails**.

## Relevant Documentation

**On count mismatches:**
> "Please make sure that the number of installer URL(s) included matches the number of existing installer nodes in the manifest you are updating. Otherwise, the command will fail."

This requirement exists "to ensure that we can deterministically update each installer node with the correct matching installer url provided."

**On matching strategy:**
The tool matches installers by analyzing "the installer architecture and installer type," with architecture determined through regex pattern matching on the URL. If that fails, it downloads and analyzes the actual binary.

The tool won't silently remove unmatched installers or proceed with partial updates—it enforces a one-to-one correspondence between provided URLs and existing manifest entries.

content.text:
No — and that's the catch I flagged earlier, now confirmed with specifics. There is no manifest file in this repo. The `winget` job doesn't build a manifest from a local template: `wingetcreate update` downloads the latest **published** manifest from `microsoft/winget-pkgs`, patches the version and installer URLs into it, and opens the PR from that.

I verified both sides of the mismatch:

- The currently published manifest (29.26.0) has **four** installer entries: msix x64/arm64 plus msi x64/arm64 (the MSI entries carry `ProductCode`s, the MSIX ones `PackageFamilyName`).
- The winget-create docs are explicit that the URL count must match the existing installer nodes one-to-one: "Otherwise, the command will fail." It won't silently drop the unmatched MSI entries.

So with our change (two URLs against four nodes), the winget step of the first MSI-less release will fail — deterministically, not with stale entries as I first guessed.

The clean way out is a one-time manual submission for that first release: copy the latest manifest in winget-pkgs, bump the version, update the two MSIX URLs and their `InstallerSha256` values, and delete the two MSI installer nodes. Once that msix-only version is merged, the automated job matches two-to-two again and every subsequent release flows through untouched. Doing it at release time (rather than editing 29.26.0 now) is preferable, because the current MSI URLs are still valid for the published version and moderators are more receptive to installer changes arriving with a new version.

I can't prepare that PR yet — it needs the release URLs and hashes that won't exist until the next release is cut. If you'd like, I can note it in the release workflow as a comment above the winget job so it isn't forgotten, but otherwise the repo side is complete.
